names.japanese_name: ヒドロキシプロピルメチルセルロース

classification.sub_class: emulsifier, stabilizer, film-former, gelling agent, coating agent

natural_occurrence.description: HPMC is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose, created by chemically modifying plant cellulose with both methyl and hydroxypropyl groups. It's essentially chemically processed wood fiber or cotton.

manufacturing.description: Produced by treating cellulose with alkali, then reacting it with methyl chloride and propylene oxide. This dual substitution gives HPMC a unique combination of properties - it's water-soluble, forms clear gels, and creates flexible films. The ratio of methyl to hydroxypropyl groups can be varied to create different grades.

other_uses.cosmetics: Thickener and film-former in shampoos, lotions, and cosmetics.
other_uses.industrial: Construction (tile adhesive, cement modifier, wallpaper paste), paints, ceramics, textiles.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Vegetarian capsule shells (gelatin alternative), tablet binder and coating, ophthalmic solutions, controlled-release matrix.
other_uses.household: Tile grout, paint thickener, personal lubricants.

eu.conditions: Widely permitted in most food categories; considered safe cellulose derivative.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ヒドロキシプロピルメチルセルロース

commonly_found_in: Vegetarian and vegan supplement capsules (gelatin-free alternative)
Gluten-free baked goods (improves texture and moisture retention)
fun_facts: HPMC makes vegetarian capsules possible - it's the plant-based alternative to gelatin capsules, allowing vegetarians and vegans to take supplements
Like all celluloses, it's basically chemically modified wood pulp or plant fiber - you're eating processed sawdust!
The same chemical in your vegetarian supplement capsule is also used in tile grout and cement - both benefit from its water-retention and adhesive properties
It's completely indigestible and passes through your system as dietary fiber with zero calories
HPMC has thermal gelation properties like methyl cellulose - it forms a gel when heated and becomes liquid again when cooled, useful for making crispy fried coatings
